one_line_takeaway: Vul-R2 shows that a reasoning-first recipe -- synthetic CoT SFT plus two-stage curriculum RLVR with a critic-LLM verifiable reward -- substantially improves C/C++ vulnerability-patch generation (PrimeVul EM 24.83% vs 13.56%, +49 fixes) and transfers to SVEN without retraining, though absolute repair rates remain low.
key_finding: On PrimeVul, Vul-R2 reaches EM 24.83%, Success 108, CodeBLEU 46.17, beating the strongest baseline (Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct SFT: EM 13.56, Success 59) by +11.27 EM points and 49 additional fixed vulnerabilities. Transferred to SVEN with no further training it reaches EM 39.13%, Success 144, CodeBLEU 61.40 (vs Qwen2.5-14B SFT EM 30.35 / Success 112), i.e. +8.78 EM points and +32 fixes -- evidencing cross-dataset generalization. Ablations show the reasoning data (DARL/RAC) matters most (removing it drops PrimeVul EM by 11.27 points to 13.56), while CVRT adds a further ~2.53 EM points; CVRT beats plain GRPO (24.83 vs 22.53 EM) and the critic LLM contributes ~1.61 EM points. Reasoning data augmented with the code domain helps (best) whereas math-domain data does not. The authors also report an emergent 'aha moment' (rising response length ~300->500 tokens in the hard stage, more 'verify'/'ensure' markers).
core_contribution: Vul-R2 is a reasoning LLM for automated vulnerability repair (AVR) of C/C++ code, built on Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct-1M. Its own contribution (distinct from cited prior AVR/RLVR work) is a two-part training recipe: (1) a Domain-Aware Reasoning Learning (DARL) module that uses a 'Reasoning Answer Construction' prompt (with CWE domain knowledge, repair-step guidance, and inverse verification) driven by OpenAI-o3 to synthesize chain-of-thought repair traces, then filters them via an LLM-as-judge plus rule-based checks, and SFTs on a mix of these vulnerability CoT traces and CodeForces algorithmic problems; and (2) a Curriculum-based Verifiable Rewarded Training (CVRT) module that applies RLVR (REINFORCE++-style objective with KL regularization) in two stages -- an 'easy' stage reframing repair as multiple-choice selection among candidate patches, and a 'hard' stage of open-ended patch generation -- using a critic-LLM answer reward plus a character-level similarity term and a format reward. The paper positions itself as the first work to train a reasoning LLM via RLVR specifically for vulnerability repair, addressing the absence of verifiable intermediate feedback in the repair process.
threat_model: This is a defensive/remediation security paper, not an attack or adversarial-robustness paper. It addresses the software-security problem of automatically generating correct patches for known, labeled vulnerabilities (real-world CWE-tagged C/C++ flaws such as integer overflow, NULL pointer dereference, resource management errors) to reduce the manual effort of security experts. There is no adversarial threat model over the model itself (no poisoning/evasion/prompt-injection analysis); the 'adversary' is implicitly the latent vulnerability in existing code. The setup assumes the vulnerable code region is already localized (patches substitute code between // bug_start and // bug_end markers) and that a ground-truth human patch exists for training/evaluation.
limitations: Authors' own stated threats: (1) evaluated only on C/C++ (PrimeVul, SVEN); Java (Vul4J) excluded due to too few samples for RLVR. (2) All reasoning samples <4,096 tokens, so long code contexts get truncated and may be mis-repaired. (3) Foundation-model choice limited to the Qwen-2.5 series. Reviewer-noted caveats: the headline '11.27%'/'8.78%' improvements are absolute percentage-point gains in EM (24.83 vs 13.56), not relative improvements -- the paper's phrasing ('outperforms by 11.27% for EM') can read as relative. Absolute EM remains low (24.83% on PrimeVul), so ~75% of vulnerabilities are still not exactly repaired. Evaluation depends on exact/near-exact match to a single human ground-truth patch, which may undercount semantically-correct alternative fixes; the critic that supplies training reward is the same Qwen2.5-14B family, raising a mild self-evaluation concern. Bug region is pre-localized, so end-to-end detection+localization+repair is not evaluated. Injection-type CWEs (0/4) and scarce categories are not improved.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (real-world C/C++, >140 CWE categories, aggregated from BigVul/CVEFixes/CrossVul/DiverseVul; 3,789 train / 480 val / 435 test)
SVEN (manually vetted C/C++, 384 vulnerabilities, 9 CWEs, 94.0% labeling accuracy; used as test-only, no training)
CodeForces algorithmic problems (Dcode; code-domain SFT augmentation)
AMC and AIME (math-domain data; used only in a reasoning-data ablation, found not to help)
quantitative_results: PrimeVul best baseline (Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct SFT): EM 13.56%, Success 59, CodeBLEU 39.20 -> Vul-R2 gain +11.27 EM points and +49 fixed vulnerabilities
SVEN Vul-R2 (no additional training): EM 39.13%, Success 144, CodeBLEU 61.40
SVEN Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct SFT: EM 30.35%, Success 112 -> Vul-R2 gain +8.78 EM points and +32 fixes
Ablation PrimeVul: w/o RAC reasoning data EM 13.56% (-11.27), w/o CVRT EM 22.30% (-2.53)
RLVR variants PrimeVul EM: CVRT (full) 24.83 vs GRPO 22.53 (-2.30) vs w/o Critic LLM 23.22 (-1.61) vs zero-RLVR (no SFT) 23.67 (-1.16)
Reasoning-data domain PrimeVul EM: w/o Reason 13.56, w/ Reason 17.24, w/ Reason&Math 18.16, w/ Reason&Code 22.30 (best)

Per-CWE exact matches (Vul-R2): Numeric Error 10/35 (28.57%), NULL Pointer Dereference 11/39 (28.21%), Resource Management Error 59/229 (25.76%), Improper Access Control 6/19 (31.58%), Injection 0/4 (no improvement)
Hyperparameters: best at N=16 rollouts and 250 RLVR training steps; prompt/response max length 4,096 tokens
baselines_compared: Also compared internally: Vul-R2* (RLVR only, no SFT) as an ablation variant
